text: Interesting points. My reactions are generally negative. I understand more about technology than most and I don't know how it works when you get to the electron level (my physics and chemistry knowledge stopped after high school). Technology should be easy. If it's complex it's because we don't understand it, and we're doing it wrong. We should fight complexity. A picture is worth a thousand words--show me don't tell me. We are not experts and we don't know what we're doing, but we try our best :slightly_smiling_face:

text: Can anyone think of any examples of data structure visualisers that allow you to change the presentation of the nodes of the data structure? Like ‘tree’, ‘table’, ‘xml’, ‘bytes’, ‘image’. I have some ideas for a binary data visualiser like this (driven by a data description language I’m making), I’m just curious about prior art on this kind of thing. Examples don’t have to be for binary data formats specifically though. It’s a UI I swear I’ve seen in the past…

@denik on Twitter (creator of Vimsical) made something like this and demoed it to me privately. Send him a DM and he'd probably be happy to talk about it. It was a JS framework for rendering arbitrary tree structures with arbitrary presentations based on the nesting or data of each node. A bit like React but much looser / more open.

This is almost exactly how my Beads language is designed. It uses a tracked global state, with basically no local state, no NEW, no DISPOSE. Solves a ton of problems. Basically the opposite of OOP.
This is almost exactly how my Beads language is designed. It uses a tracked global state, with basically no local state, no NEW, no DISPOSE. Solves a ton of problems. Basically the opposite of OOP.
nice
nice

Koji solves an incredible of very difficult problems that programmers are faced with. It is a very commendable project, one of best i have seen in a long time. Just handling recertification for free using the certs that you renew every 30 days saves people a lot of money (several hundred dollars for code signing certs from most companies, because there is a monopoly now with all the acquisitions of certificate issuing authorities). Trying to develop inside a browser with the inability to write the local hard drive makes software development almost impossible, so their connection to a server spun up from a pool is very clever.  I made a lot of iphone apps, and waiting 2 weeks for approval, not to mention the $99/year is an impediment of sorts.

the one thing i don't like about it is that you are using JS as the coding language. JS is not a good programming language. It is full of traps for the unwary. Anyway Koji allows people to use so many services that would take months to learn, so in that sense it is a return to a gentler time.

Firefox is working on RR in the browser (WebReplay). We hope launch and go to a small beta early next year. <https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Mozilla/Projects/WebReplayRoadmap>

attachments.title: WebReplayRoadmap
attachments.text: Web Replay is an experimental technology which allows Firefox tabs to record their behavior so that it can be replayed and rewound later.  Replay is at a high fidelity: JavaScript, DOM, and CSS behavior all replay exactly as they did while recording.  Currently only macOS nightly builds can use Web Replay, and some web features like media elements are disabled.  After a couple years of development, performance and stability are in a good place, and this technology is ready to use.

The roadmap is basically
1. support local debugging
2. support cloud debugging where recordings are uploaded and shared
3. support ci environments where recordings are attached to failing tests
4. support production where recordings are attached to user exceptions

:wave: Hey, I'm working on Firefox DevTools. I'd love folks thoughts on WebReplay, an in browser RR environment. <https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Mozilla/Projects/WebReplayRoadmap>

attachments.title: WebReplayRoadmap
attachments.text: Web Replay is an experimental technology which allows Firefox tabs to record their behavior so that it can be replayed and rewound later.  Replay is at a high fidelity: JavaScript, DOM, and CSS behavior all replay exactly as they did while recording.  Currently only macOS nightly builds can use Web Replay, and some web features like media elements are disabled.  After a couple years of development, performance and stability are in a good place, and this technology is ready to use.

Those interested in the future of coding may also be interested in the past... Just wanted to drop a note about the upcoming HOPL IV conference (history of programming languages), chaired by Guy Steele and Richard Gabriel in London, June 14-16, 2020. This is the 4th edition of a series started in 1978 and run every 10-15 years so should be a special event. I am not involved and have no stake, just spreading the word. <https://hopl4.sigplan.org> (prior: <http://research.ihost.com/hopl/HOPL.html>)

I'm guessing an important part of what he's saying is that a (properly namespaced) global state is very debuggable, inspectable, loggable, persistable, reversable and many other -ables. Local state is invisible and resists all these -ables. Code without local state is like functional transformers on the global state with predictable outcomes, much like shell pipelines. Is that what he's saying?

<@UJUQESQ1Z> we don't have any visualizations with sizzle yet, but our theory is that all data structures are a kind of tree, tree notation is the simplest 2d text representation of trees, and so you can use Tree notation as the basic building block to then build all kinds of visualizations of trees. here's an example that takes a tree and can spit out json/xml/csv/yaml, etc

<@UJUQESQ1Z> we don't have any visualizations with sizzle yet, but our theory is that all data structures are a kind of tree, tree notation is the simplest 2d text representation of trees, and so you can use Tree notation as the basic building block to then build all kinds of visualizations of trees. here's an example that takes a tree and can spit out json/xml/csv/yaml, etc
<http://jtree.treenotation.org/sandbox/>
<http://jtree.treenotation.org/sandbox/>
In this one: <http://jtree.treenotation.org/designer/> click any demo languages and then click "Explain", and you'll get a visualization of your tree as a 2D spreadsheet

has anyone ever read a post on good and bad strategies to get to a state with good tooling for a new language? i've learned no matter how great your new language X is, unless it's magically better than old language Y + Ytools, it's going to be worse than Y+YTools. So overcoming that hump is a big challenge, and I'm wondering if there are strategic or tactical methods that work better than others

<@UCUSW7WVD> only just now got to read the 3 links in your first comment, and they're all rather interesting. Looks like I am mostly a Camp 2 conservative who embraces evolution, though I have aspects of all sides I think :slightly_smiling_face: Unlike the 3 articles, I am afraid I do not follow how Post-Modernism relates to software, so no idea there.

If anything, seems like this Slack has a vast majority of "camp 1" people, which in turn seems to overlap with "The Right Thing" and being anti-evolutional (instead of making existing programming tools better, want the world to jump to their new vision of programming).. that would, at least from the perspective of those 3 links, not bode well for this Slack :wink:
